title: The U.S. and Iran Signed a Memorandum of Misunderstanding
gist: A former U.N. diplomat performs a clause-by-clause legal reading of the two-page U.S.-Iran Hormuz cease-fire text and concludes it is vague and asymmetric enough that Iran's assertion of managerial control over the strait — and the U.S.'s subsequent strikes — are each arguably compliant with the letter of the agreement, meaning the collapse traces to sloppy drafting rather than bad faith alone.

themes.theme: The agreement was structurally unfit for purpose

insights.claim: The signed U.S.-Iran agreement runs just over two pages and is vague, ambiguous, and often opaque, in sharp contrast to serious cease-fire texts such as the 259-page 2005 Sudan Comprehensive Peace Agreement, which includes a 50-page annex covering cease-fire and security implementation alone.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: Document length and specificity is a leading indicator of an agreement's durability; a two-page text on a conflict this volatile should be read as inherently fragile regardless of the parties' stated intentions.
insights.locator: By any measure, this process produced a deeply flawed text

insights.claim: The agreement is mislabeled from its opening words: calling it a 'memorandum of understanding' is meaningless since an MOU by definition is nonbinding, yet the document plainly was intended to bind both parties' conduct.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: A naming/drafting error that presages the substantive ambiguity to follow — a red flag investors and analysts should treat as an early signal of low negotiating rigor.
insights.locator: Its very first words portend problems to come

insights.claim: Elements normally considered essential to a cease-fire agreement — monitoring and dispute-resolution mechanisms — are either entirely missing or addressed so briefly as to be almost absent from the U.S.-Iran text.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: Without enforcement or arbitration machinery, any dispute over interpretation (which the text invites) has no path to resolution short of renewed conflict.
insights.locator: Elements that would normally be considered crucial

insights.claim: In the 2020 U.S.-Taliban agreement, ambiguous language promising release of 'up to five thousand (5,000) prisoners' led the Taliban to escalate attacks specifically to capture more prisoners eligible for release — a precedent for how vague phrasing in conflict agreements produces perverse, violence-inducing incentives.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: A direct historical analog warning that ambiguous cease-fire drafting doesn't just risk confusion — it can actively incentivize renewed violence by the ambiguity's beneficiary.
insights.locator: In the 2020 U.S.-Taliban agreement, for example

themes.theme: Asymmetric obligations drove the strait dispute

insights.claim: Paragraph 5 of the agreement only requires Iran to 'make arrangements using its best efforts for the safe passage of commercial vessels, with no charge for 60 days only' — a much weaker and vaguer commitment than paragraph 4's requirement that the U.S. 'fully end' its blockade of Iranian ports, and critically, it does not require mutual agreement or consent on how those arrangements are implemented.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: The textual asymmetry gave Iran legal room to unilaterally impose a permit system, which is exactly what happened — a case study in how one-sided drafting creates the very dispute it was meant to prevent.
insights.quote: The Islamic Republic of Iran will make arrangements using its best efforts for the safe passage of commercial vessels, with no charge for 60 days only.
insights.locator: The key passage is from paragraph 5

insights.claim: Iran created a Persian Gulf Strait Authority (PGSA) requiring ships to obtain a transit permit and use Iran's approved corridors through its territorial waters — arrangements that, however objectionable, do not appear to violate the agreement's text, since the agreement never requires Iran to let every commercial ship through unimpeded.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: Iran's actions, widely portrayed in the West as a violation, have a coherent textual defense — investors assessing Hormuz risk should not assume Iran is simply acting in bad faith outside the deal's terms.
insights.locator: Iran does indeed appear to have made arrangements

insights.claim: The agreement's ban on force applies only between the two signatories (paragraph 1) and says nothing about Iranian use of force against third-party commercial vessels, so Iran's strikes on ships transiting outside its approved corridor (without a PGSA permit) are arguably compliant with, or at least not explicitly prohibited by, the agreement's text.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: A critical loophole: a 'cease-fire' that only restricts force between signatories leaves third-party shipping exposed, a structural gap other future agreements should close explicitly.
insights.locator: The agreement also does not explicitly prohibit any and all Iranian use of force

insights.claim: The U.S.-led Joint Maritime Information Center repeatedly advised commercial ships to use a non-Iranian-approved transit corridor after the agreement, and OFAC sanctions against the PGSA were never lifted, with OFAC warning that anyone cooperating with the 'so-called strait authority' faced sanctions risk — active U.S. efforts to deny Iran managerial control that likely provoked Iranian retaliation.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: The U.S. was not a passive victim of Iranian overreach — it took its own text-compliant-but-provocative countermeasures, meaning any future Hormuz stability effort must address both sides' incentives, not just Iran's.
insights.quote: Anyone cooperating with the so-called strait authority

insights.claim: Trump almost immediately violated the agreement's clear and explicit prohibition on threats of force against Iran, which may have reinforced Iran's perception that the U.S. was acting in bad faith and justified its own subsequent actions.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: Identifies the U.S. side, specifically Trump's own conduct, as an early and clear-cut breach that undermines any U.S. narrative painting Iran as the sole violator.
insights.locator: The U.S. may well have encouraged such a perception

themes.theme: Outlook for Hormuz and future negotiations

insights.claim: Even if the current agreement is deeply flawed and, if faithfully implemented, could destabilize the region, Iran's leadership likely genuinely believes it upheld its end of the bargain while the U.S. ignored significant elements of it — a perception, right or wrong, that will cast a long shadow over any future effort to reopen the strait.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: Future Hormuz negotiations must first address Iran's belief that the prior deal was broken by Washington, or renewed talks will stall on trust before reaching substance.
insights.locator: Given all the circumstances, it is therefore entirely plausible

insights.claim: There is no reason to believe the U.S. or any other power is both willing and able to force the Strait of Hormuz open without Iranian consent, meaning any stable resolution to the crisis must eventually return to the negotiating table.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: Rules out a purely military reopening of Hormuz as a durable solution — investors modeling shipping-lane or oil-transit risk should treat renewed negotiation, not escalation, as the base case path to resolution.
insights.locator: There is no reason to believe that the U.S. or any other power

insights.claim: The agreement's provisions on Lebanon are not binding on Israel and give the U.S. wide latitude, while its temporary sanctions-relief provisions leave in place OFAC measures that in practice supersede much of that relief — suggesting Iranian negotiators also misunderstood key elements of the English-language text.
insights.speaker: David Raikow
insights.so_what: Both sides appear to have negotiated a text they didn't fully understand, reinforcing that the deal's failure is rooted in drafting incompetence as much as strategic bad faith.
insights.locator: There is reason to believe, moreover, that Iranian leadership
